title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
Evolution of ULIPs over the Years
Meta description: ULIPs or Unit Linked Insurance Plans have evolved a lot over the years. Check out how today’s ULIPs offer a variety of benefits.
Keywords: ulip policy, ulip plan meaning, what is ulip plan, ulip funds
The only thing that is a constant in the Indian financial sector is change. All the financial products we have today have undergone massive changes since they were each first introduced in the Indian markets. Insurance policies are no different. The insurance sector has witnessed massive growth over the past few decades.
Insurance policies have also evolved significantly in the years gone by. Today, insurance covers offer a wide range of benefits aside from just a life cover. A ULIP policy is one such kind of life insurance plan that has transformed since it was first launched in India.
To truly understand how much ULIP policies have evolved, let us begin at the fundamentals and trace the history of ULIPs to arrive at where we are today.
What is a ULIP plan?
ULIPs or Unit Linked Insurance Plans are a popular kind of life insurance plans. They combine the benefit of insurance and investment for the policyholder. The insurance component offers a life cover, so that in case the insured person passes away during the policy term, the insurer pays out death benefits to the nominee assigned.
As for the investment component, you can invest in market linked ULIP funds when you purchase this kind of a life insurance policy. ULIP funds include equity funds, debt funds or a mix of the two.
Based on how the market performs over the policy term, your investment returns will be calculated and paid out to you at maturity. This is how a ULIP policy gives you the benefit of insurance and investment.
When were ULIPs first introduced?
Although there are several insurance providers today who offer Unit Linked Insurance Plans, ULIPs were first introduced in India by the Unit Trust of India (UTI) in 1971. They were launched as investment vehicles that also offered a guaranteed life cover. However, back then, ULIPs were not widely accepted due to a number of reasons.
Here is a closer look at the top factors that affected the slow adoption of ULIPs during the initial stages.
Low inclination to take on market risks
Back then, when ULIPs were first introduced, the average Indian retail investor was not very keen on taking on market risks. So, since ULIPs were market-linked products, many investors were wary of adding these policies to their portfolio despite the guaranteed life cover.
High front loaded charges
ULIPs had steep charges when they were first introduced. This included charges for distributors as well. And it also dissuaded many people from purchasing the Unit Linked Insurance Plans introduced by UTI.
Lack of transparency
In the 70s, there was also not much known about ULIPs. The lack of transparency and the overall lack of awareness led to many misconceptions, which further stalled the popularity of Unit Linked Insurance Plans.
The evolution of ULIPs over the years
Due to all the reasons listed above, ULIPs were not embraced by the retail investors in India right away. The need of the hour was a massive transformation in this product segment. And this was why the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI), the insurance regulator, stepped up and introduced several norms and regulations around 2010.
These regulations were introduced with a view to make ULIPs more investor-friendly and to streamline the ULIP policy segment in the Indian insurance market. Here is a closer look at the new norms that the IRDAI introduced to transform ULIPs for the better.
Spreading of charges over a longer period
Earlier, ULIPs had a lock-in period of 3 years, which was why most of the charges were concentrated over such a short span of time. The IRDAI then increased the lock-in period of Unit Linked Insurance Plans to 5 years.
Capping of commissions
Prior to these reforms, distributor commissions were around 50% of the premium paid in the first year. This was reduced to around 7% to 10% by the IRDAI. This increased the amount of money that was invested in the policyholder’s ULIP funds.
Increase in life cover
Another major regulation that the IRDAI introduced to make ULIPs more investor-friendly was about the life covered offered by these plans. Following this change, ULIPs had to offer a minimum life cover that was at least ten times the premium charged in the first year.
These changes made Unit Linked Insurance Plans evolve into more attractive and beneficial investment options for the average policyholder.
Unit Linked Insurance Plans today
Today, after the many changes initiated by the IRDAI and adopted by the insurance providers, Unit Linked Insurance Plans are highly beneficial investment vehicles that can bring a variety of advantages to your portfolio.
Here is a closer look at what a typical ULIP policy has to offer today.
Investments that match your risk profile
ULIP is a unique investment option because it is one of the few vehicles that are suited for all kinds of investor profiles. Whether you are a risk-averse investor or an aggressive investor, ULIPs can fit right into your portfolio. Today, you can choose from a wide range of equity and debt investment funds to invest in via your Unit Linked Insurance Plan. You can also invest in a mix of ULIP funds that balance both equity and debt and optimise your risk exposure.
Tax benefits
ULIPs have also evolved to offer tax benefits to the policyholder. Your ULIP premium is deductible from your total taxable income up to Rs. 1.5 lakhs per financial year. This is as per section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961.
But that’s not all. The amount you receive as returns from your ULIP are also tax-free as per section 10(10D) of the Act. This is conditional to the fact that your annual ULIP premiums should be Rs. 2.5 lakh or lower. Otherwise, your gains over Rs. 1 lakh are taxable as long term capital gains.
Fund switching option
ULIPs also offer fund switching options. This effectively means that each policy year, you can change the mix of ULIP funds in your portfolio to meet your evolving financial needs or to keep up with market movements.
So, for instance, if you are approaching retirement, you can reduce your equity exposure and increase debt investments. Alternatively, if the markets are performing well, you can increase your investments in equity ULIP funds. Most insurers offer this feature without any cost or at a nominal charge.
Top-ups
If you receive a bonus or come by an inheritance that leaves you with some extra funds, you can now use this money to enhance your corpus, thanks to the top-up facility that ULIPs today offer. This essentially means that you can invest more in your ULIP each year. It will result in an increase in the premium you pay towards your ULIP plan. So, with the top-up feature, you can use any additional funds to achieve your life goals faster.
Conclusion
This sums up the interesting and insightful evolution of ULIP policies over the past few decades. The insurance sector is dynamic and constantly evolving. And the insurance regulator IRDAI consistently oversees the functioning of the insurance providers and introduces investor-friendly measures in a timely manner. The end goal is to ensure that all of India is adequately insured.

https://www.businesstoday.in/magazine/insurance/story/ulips-ratings-after-norms-changed-in-2010-134650-2014-02-21
full_pdf_text:
Evolution of ULIPs over the Years The only thing that is a constant in the Indian financial sector is change. All the financial products we have today have undergone massive changes since they were each first introduced in the Indian markets. Insurance policies are no different. The insurance sector has witnessed massive growth over the past few decades. Insurance policies have also evolved significantly in the years gone by. Today, insurance covers offer a wide range of benefits aside from just a life cover. A ULIP policy is one such kind of life insurance plan that has transformed since it was first launched in India. To truly understand how much ULIP policies have evolved, let us begin with the fundamentals and trace the history of ULIPs to arrive at where we are today. What is a ULIP plan? ULIPs or Unit Linked Insurance Plans are a popular kind of life insurance plans. They combine the benefit of insurance and investment for the policyholder. The insurance component offers a life cover, so that in case the insured person passes away during the policy term, the insurer pays out death benefits to the nominee assigned. As for the investment component, you can invest in market linked ULIP funds when you purchase this kind of a life insurance policy. ULIP funds include equity funds, debt funds or a mix of the two. Based on how the market performs over the policy term, your investment returns will be calculated and paid out to you at maturity. This is how a ULIP policy gives you the benefit of insurance and investment. When ULIPs were first introduced? Although there are several insurance providers today who offer Unit Linked Insurance Plans, ULIPs were first introduced in India by the Unit Trust of India (UTI) in 1971. They were launched as investment vehicles that also offered a guaranteed life cover. However, back then, ULIPs were not widely accepted due to a number of reasons. Here is a closer look at the top factors that affected the slow adoption of ULIPs during the initial stages. ● High front loaded charges ULIPs had steep charges when they were first introduced. This included charges for distributors as well. And it also dissuaded many people from purchasing the Unit Linked Insurance Plans introduced by UTI. ● Lack of awareness In the 70s, there was also not much awareness about ULIPs. The overall lack of awareness led to many misconceptions, which further stalled the popularity of Unit Linked Insurance Plans.
The evolution of ULIPs over the years Due to some of the reasons listed above, ULIPs were not embraced by the retail investors in India right away. The need of the hour was a massive transformation in this product segment. And this was why the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI), the insurance regulator, stepped up and introduced several norms and regulations around 2010. These regulations were introduced with a view to make ULIPs more investor-friendly and to streamline the ULIP policy segment in the Indian insurance market. Unit Linked Insurance Plans today Today, with many changes initiated by the IRDAI and adopted by the insurance providers, Unit Linked Insurance Plans can be considered as beneficial investment vehicles that can bring a variety of advantages to your portfolio. Here is a closer look at what a typical ULIP policy has to offer today. ● Investments that match your risk profile ULIP is a unique investment option because it is one of the few vehicles that are suited for all kinds of investor profiles. Whether you are a risk-averse investor or an aggressive investor, ULIPs can fit right into your portfolio. Today, you can choose from a wide range of equity and debt investment funds to invest in via your Unit Linked Insurance Plan. You can also invest in a mix of aforesaid funds that balance both equity and debt and optimise your risk exposure. ● Tax benefits ULIPs have also evolved to provide tax advantages to policyholders. Your ULIP premium payment is deducted from your total taxable income up to a maximum of Rs. 1.5 lakhs each fiscal year. This is in accordance with Section 80C of the Income Tax Act of 1961 (the Act), subject to provisions stated therein. The amount you receive as returns from your ULIP is likewise tax-free if you meet the standards outlined in section 10(10D) of the Act. In case of high-value ULIP, the tax treatment will be governed basis whether such policy qualifies to be unit of an ‘equity-oriented fund’. If the fund invests minimum 65% of the total proceeds in equity shares of listed domestic company or 90% of total proceeds in units of another listed fund, which in-turn invest 90% of proceeds in equity shares of listed domestic company, in such a case it shall be treated as equity- oriented fund. Such investment thresholds are required to be satisfied throughout the term of such insurance policy.
Thus, if the above investment thresholds are satisfied, the high-value ULIP shall be treated as unit of equity-oriented fund. Else, ULIP shall be treated as unit of debt-oriented fund. The classification of capital gains between long-term and short-term and the applicable tax rate shall be determined as follows as per the Income Tax Act, 1961.
Particulars Equity oriented ULIP Debt oriented ULIP
LTCG 10% (without indexation) u/s. 112A
20% (without indexation) u/s. 112
STCG 15% u/s. 111A Slab rates
Holding period
1 year 3 years
● Fund switching option ULIPs also offer fund switching options. This effectively means that each policy year, you can change the mix of market-linked funds in your portfolio to meet your evolving financial needs or to keep up with market movements. So, for instance, if you are approaching retirement, you may reduce your equity exposure and increase debt investments. Alternatively, if the markets are performing well, you may increase your investments in equity funds. Most insurers offer this feature without any cost or at a nominal charge. ● Top-ups If you receive a bonus or come by an inheritance that leaves you with some extra funds, you can now use this money to enhance your corpus, thanks to the top-up facility that ULIPs today offer subject to policy terms & conditions. This essentially means that you can invest more in your ULIP each year. It will result in an increase in the invested amount you pay towards your ULIP plan. So, with the top-up feature, you can use any additional funds to achieve your life goals. Conclusion This sums up the interesting and insightful evolution of ULIP policies over the past few decades. The insurance sector is dynamic and constantly evolving. The end goal is to ensure that all of India is adequately insured.
Source https://www.dnaindia.com/personal-finance/column-evolution-of-ulips-over-the-years-2802664 https://www.businesstoday.in/magazine/insurance/story/ulips-ratings-after-norms-changed-in-2010- 134650-2014-02-21 Disclaimer: In this policy, the investment risk in investment portfolio is borne by the policyholder. Investment in ULIPs is subject to risks associated with the capital markets. The policy holder is solely responsible for his/her decisions while investing in ULIPs.The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any
investment decisions.
